examples.title: Calculate Network Latency Components
examples.description: Algorithm to compute total one-way latency across multiple network hops
examples.code:
PROCEDURE CalculateLatency(Hops[], Bandwidth, PacketSize)
 totalLatency ← 0
 FOR EACH hop IN Hops
 // Propagation delay: distance / signal speed
 propDelay ← hop.distance / hop.signalSpeed
 // Transmission delay: packet size / bandwidth
 transDelay ← PacketSize / hop.bandwidth
 // Processing delay (typical router processing)
 procDelay ← 0.001 // 1 millisecond
 // Queuing delay (random, estimate based on congestion)
 queueDelay ← hop.congestionFactor * 0.005
 hopLatency ← propDelay + transDelay + procDelay + queueDelay
 totalLatency ← totalLatency + hopLatency
 END FOR
 RETURN totalLatency
END PROCEDURE
examples.explanation: This algorithm calculates latency by summing four delay components for each hop: propagation (travel time), transmission (push time), processing (router overhead), and queuing (congestion wait). Real networks would measure these dynamically.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n = number of hops

examples.title: Determine Optimal Network Topology
examples.description: Decision algorithm to select appropriate topology based on network requirements
examples.code:
PROCEDURE SelectTopology(NodeCount, Budget, Reliability, Scalability)
 IF Reliability = "HIGH" AND Budget = "HIGH" THEN
 RETURN "Full Mesh"
 ELSE IF Reliability = "HIGH" AND Budget = "LOW" THEN
 RETURN "Partial Mesh"
 ELSE IF NodeCount < 10 AND Budget = "LOW" THEN
 RETURN "Star (for P2P)"
 ELSE IF NodeCount < 50 THEN
 RETURN "Star with Ring Backbone"
 ELSE IF Scalability = "HIGH" THEN
 RETURN "Tree Topology"
 ELSE
 RETURN "Star Topology"
 END IF
END PROCEDURE

// Example usage:
// SelectTopology(100, "MEDIUM", "MEDIUM", "HIGH") → Returns "Tree Topology"
examples.explanation: This decision tree helps network designers choose appropriate topology based on three key factors: number of nodes (scalability requirements), budget constraints, and reliability needs. Full mesh provides best reliability but highest cost.

examples.title: Calculate Channel Capacity (Nyquist & Shannon)
examples.description: Functions to compute maximum data rate using Nyquist and Shannon theorems
examples.code:
// Nyquist Theorem: C = 2B × log₂(M)
FUNCTION NyquistCapacity(BandwidthHz, SignalLevels)
 maxBitsPerSymbol ← LOG2(SignalLevels)
 capacity ← 2 * BandwidthHz * maxBitsPerSymbol
 RETURN capacity // in bits per second
END FUNCTION

// Shannon Capacity: C = B × log₂(1 + S/N)
FUNCTION ShannonCapacity(BandwidthHz, SignalToNoiseRatioDB)
 // Convert dB to linear ratio
 snRatio ← 10 ^ (SignalToNoiseRatioDB / 10)
 capacity ← BandwidthHz * LOG2(1 + snRatio)
 RETURN capacity // in bits per second
END FUNCTION

// Example: B=4000Hz, S/N=30dB, M=2
BEGIN
 nyquistResult ← NyquistCapacity(4000, 2)
 shannonResult ← ShannonCapacity(4000, 30)
 PRINT "Nyquist: " + nyquistResult + " bps"
 PRINT "Shannon: " + shannonResult + " bps"
END
examples.explanation: Two fundamental theorems for channel capacity: Nyquist limits ideal noiseless channels based on signal levels, Shannon accounts for noise in real channels. The lower of the two results is the practical maximum data rate.
